topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an oppressive intensity, reflecting off the polished wood of the table. My hands were slick with sweat, gripping the worn edges of the printed essay as if it were a lifeline. Every time I glanced at the damning email notification flashing on my laptop screen, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. The accusation hung in the air, thick and suffocating, a tangible weight pressing down on my chest. I kept my gaze fixed on the textbook, tracing the meaningless diagrams, desperately trying to anchor myself to something real. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, each inhale feeling like a conscious effort against the rising tide of panic. I could feel the heat creeping up my neck, and my palms were trembling so violently I had to consciously press them flat against the desk to stop the shaking from escalating.

[story 2]

"Are you certain about this, Alex?" Professor Davies’ voice was dangerously level, devoid of any warmth. The room seemed to shrink around me, the silence amplifying the frantic thumping in my ears. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges, focusing only on the intricate pattern of the carpet beneath my feet. Every movement felt exaggerated, clumsy. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the echoing hallway, but my feet were rooted to the spot. I could feel a strange pressure behind my eyes, a dizzying sensation that made the words on the page swim. I tried to articulate a defense, but only a dry, rasping sound escaped my lips.

[story 3]

The door clicked shut behind me, sealing me in with the accusation. I stood by the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, each drop mirroring the turmoil inside. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that threatened to betray me entirely. I kept running my fingers through my hair, a nervous, repetitive motion that offered no comfort. The air felt thin, insufficient. When the committee members began their questioning, their collective scrutiny felt like a physical assault. I focused intently on a small smudge on the windowpane, willing myself to remain still, to keep the tremor in my hands from becoming uncontrollable. My mouth felt terribly dry, and I fought the urge to shake my head, to simply turn and flee the suffocating atmosphere.
